name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۖ لَا يَضُرُّكُم مَّن ضَلَّ إِذَا ٱهْتَدَيْتُمْ ۚ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٠٥)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Take care of your own souls. He who strays cannot hurt you if you are guided. To Allah will be the return of you all, whereat He will inform you concerning what you used to do. (105)
```

## (Commentary)

The verse commands the believers to adhere to their souls and to remain steadfast upon the path of their guidance. They should not be distressed by the straying of those among mankind who have gone astray, for Allah, the Glorified, is the Return to Whom all shall go, and He is the Judge over everyone according to their deeds. Nevertheless, the discourse is not devoid of a profound depth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ لا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ (O you who have faith! Take care of your own souls. He who strays cannot hurt you if you are guided). The word عَلَيْكُمْ (upon you) is a verbal noun meaning "adhere to" or "take care of," and أَنْفُسَكُمْ (your souls) is its object.

It is well known that straying (الضلال) and guidance (الاهتداء)—which are two opposite concepts—only manifest in the treading of a path and nowhere else. Thus, the one who adheres to the main body of the path reaches the destination to which the path leads, which is the desired goal intended by the wayfarer in his journeying. However, if he treats it with indifference and deviates from the level of the path, that is straying (الضلال), by which the intended goal is missed. Thus, the verse posits for man a path he treads and a destination he seeks; however, he may either adhere to the path and thus be guided to it, or transgress from it and thus go astray. There is no destination sought by the seeker except a happy life and a good end, without a doubt. Yet, despite this, the verse declares that Allah, the Glorified, is the Return (المرجع) to Whom everyone—the guided and the straying—shall return.

Therefore, the reward that man seeks in his journey by his primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — is only with Allah, the Glorified; the guided attain it, while the straying are deprived of it. The necessary consequence of this is that all paths trodden by the people of guidance and the paths trodden by the people of straying end at Allah, the Glorified. With Him, the Glorified, is the intended goal, even if those paths differ in bringing man to his desire, success, and prosperity, or striking him with disappointment and loss, as well as in their closeness and distance. As the Exalted said: **يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسَانُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَاقِيهِ** — "O man! You are laboring toward your Lord with [great] labor, and you will encounter Him" (84:6). And the Exalted said: **أَلَا إِنَّ حِزْبَ اللَّهِ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ** — "Behold! The confederates of Allah are indeed the felicitous" (58:22). And the Exalted said: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ كُفْرًا وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دَارَ الْبَوَارِ** — "Have you not regarded those who substituted unfaith for Allah’s blessing, and settled their people in the abode of ruin?" (14:28). And the Exalted said: **فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا لِي وَلْيُؤْمِنُوا بِي لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْشُدُونَ** — "I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me. So let them respond to Me, and let them have faith in Me, so that they may fare rightly" (2:186). And the Exalted said: **وَالَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ فِي آذَانِهِمْ وَقْرٌ وَهُوَ عَلَيْهِمْ عَمًى أُولَٰئِكَ يُنَادَوْنَ مِنْ مَكَانٍ بَعِيدٍ** — "As for those who do not believe, there is a deafness in their ears and they are blind to it. They are like those who are called from a distant place" (41:44).

The Exalted has clarified in these verses that everyone is journeying toward Him, the Glorified, a journey from which there is no escape. However, the path of some is short and contains right-guidance and prosperity, while the path of others is long, does not end in happiness, and returns nothing to its wayfarer except destruction and ruin.

In short, the verse posits for the believers and others two distinct paths that end at Allah, the Glorified. It commands the believers to occupy themselves with their own souls and to turn away from others—namely, the people of straying among mankind. They should not fall in with them nor fear their straying, for their reckoning is with their Lord, not with the believers, and the believers are not responsible for them such that their affair should distress them. Thus, the verse is close in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَغْفِرُوا لِلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ اللَّهِ لِيَجْزِيَ قَوْمًا بِمَا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ** — "Tell the faithful to forgive those who do not expect the days of Allah, so that He may recompense a people for what they used to earn" (45:14). Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted: **تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَلَكُمْ مَا كَسَبْتُمْ وَلَا تُسْأَلُونَ عَمَّا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "That was a nation that has passed: for it there is what it earned, and for you there is what you earn, and you will not be questioned about what they used to do" (2:134).

Thus, the believer must occupy himself with what concerns his own soul regarding the treading of the path of guidance. He should not be shaken by what he witnesses of the straying of people and the prevalence of sins among them, nor should that distract him or cause him to be preoccupied with them. For truth is truth even if it is abandoned, and falsehood is falsehood even if it is adopted, as the Exalted said: **قُلْ لَا يَسْتَوِي الْخَبِيثُ وَالطَّيِّبُ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ كَثْرَةُ الْخَبِيثِ فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ يَا أُولِي الْأَلْبَابِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ** — "Say, ‘The bad and the good are not equal, though the abundance of the bad may amaze you.’ So be wary of Allah, O you who possess intellects, so that you may be felicitous" (5:100). And the Exalted said: **وَلَا تَسْتَوِي الْحَسَنَةُ وَلَا السَّيِّئَةُ** — "Good and evil [conduct] are not equal" (41:34).

**His saying, the Exalted:** لا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ (He who strays cannot hurt you if you are guided), based on what has preceded, is phrased as a metonymy (الكناية). It is intended to forbid the believers from being influenced by the straying of those among mankind who have gone astray, lest that lead them to abandon the path of guidance—as if they were to say: "The present world does not support religion, nor does it permit the adoption of spiritual values; for these are merely simple traditions whose time has passed and whose people have perished." The Exalted said: **وَقَالُوا إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدَىٰ مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنَا** — "They say, ‘If we follow the guidance with you, we will be snatched away from our land’" (28:57).

Or, they might fear the influence of the straying of others upon their own guidance, thus becoming preoccupied with them and forgetting their own souls, thereby becoming like them. Indeed, the duty upon the believer is only to call toward his Lord, to enjoin the good (الأمر بالمعروف), and to forbid the evil (النهي عن المنكر)—in short, to take up the ordinary means and then entrust the matter of the results to Allah, the Glorified, for to Him belongs the entire affair. As for destroying his own soul in the path of saving others from destruction, he has not been commanded to do so, nor is he held accountable for the deeds of another, nor is he a guardian over them. Accordingly, the verse becomes similar in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَعَلَّكَ بَاخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ عَلَىٰ آثَارِهِمْ إِنْ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهَٰذَا الْحَدِيثِ أَسَفًا * إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَهَا لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا * وَإِنَّا لَجَاعِلُونَ مَا عَلَيْهَا صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا** — "You might perhaps kill yourself with grief over their footsteps if they do not believe in this discourse. Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct. And indeed We will turn whatever is on it into a barren plain" (18:6-8). And His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْآنًا سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ الْجِبَالُ أَوْ قُطِّعَتْ بِهِ الْأَرْضُ أَوْ كُلِّمَ بِهِ الْمَوْتَىٰ بَلْ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ جَمِيعًا أَفَلَمْ يَيْأَسِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ لَوْ يَشَاءُ اللَّهُ لَهَدَى النَّاسَ جَمِيعًا** — "If only it were a Quran whereby the mountains could be moved, or the earth could be traversed, or the dead could be spoken to...! Rather, all dispensation belongs to Allah. Have not the faithful yet known that had Allah wished He would have guided mankind all together?" (13:31), and similar verses.

It has become clear through this explanation that the verse does not contradict the verses of invitation (الدعوة) or the verses of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil. For the verse only forbids the believers from being preoccupied with the straying of people to the detriment of their own guidance, and from destroying themselves in the path of rescuing and saving others.

Furthermore, the invitation to Allah and enjoining the good and forbidding the evil are among the aspects of the believer's preoccupation with his own soul and his treading the path of his Lord. How could it be possible for the verse to contradict the verses of invitation and enjoining the good and forbidding the evil, or to abrogate them? Allah, the Glorified, has counted them among the distinguishing characteristics of this religion and the foundations upon which it is built, as the Exalted said: **قُلْ هَٰذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُو إِلَى اللَّهِ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي** — "Say, ‘This is my way: I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me’" (12:108). And the Exalted said: **كُنْتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ تَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَتَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ** — "You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind: you bid what is right and forbid what is wrong" (3:110).

Thus, the believer must call to Allah with insight and enjoin the good and forbid the evil as a performance of the divine obligation. It is not upon him to become agitated and destroy himself with grief, or to go to extremes in striving for the effect of that upon the souls of the people of straying; for that [burden] has been removed from him.

Since the verse has posited for the believers a path containing their guidance and for others a path whose nature is the straying of its wayfarers, and then commanded the believers in His saying: عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ (Take care of your own souls) to adhere to their souls, this contains an indication that the soul (النفس) of the believer is the very path he is commanded to tread and adhere to. For the exhortation toward a path is only consistent with the exhortation to adhere to it and the warning against abandoning it, not with adhering to the wayfarer of the path, as we observe in such verses as His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنَّ هَٰذَا صِرَاطِي مُسْتَقِيمًا فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** — "This is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way" (6:153).

Therefore, His commanding the believers to adhere to their souls in the context of exhorting them to safeguard the path of their guidance implies that the path they must tread and adhere to is their own souls. Thus, the soul of the believer is his path which he treads toward his Lord; it is the path of his guidance and that which leads him to his happiness.

The verse thus clarifies the objective aimed at collectively by other verses, such as His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَلْتَنْظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ خَبِيرٌ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ * وَلَا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَأَنْسَاهُمْ أَنْفُسَهُمْ أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْفَاسِقُونَ * لَا يَسْتَوِي أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ وَأَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ هُمُ الْفَائِزُونَ** — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for tomorrow, and be wary of Allah. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. And do not be like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget their own souls. It is they who are the transgressors. The inmates of the Fire and the inhabitants of paradise are not equal. It is the inhabitants of paradise who are the winners" (59:18-20).

These verses command that the soul should look and monitor its righteous deeds, which are its provisions for tomorrow—and the best of provisions is godwariness (التقوى). For the soul, there is a "today" and a "tomorrow," and it is in a state of journeying and movement across a distance. The goal is Allah, the Glorified, and with Him is the good reward, which is Paradise. Therefore, it must persist in the remembrance of its Lord and not forget Him, for He, the Glorified, is the Goal. Forgetting the goal entails forgetting the path; thus, whoever forgets his Lord forgets his soul, and he does not prepare for his tomorrow and the future of his journey any provisions to sustain him, which is destruction. This is the meaning of what has been narrated by both groups [Sunni and Shi'a] from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Whoever knows his soul has known his Lord."

This meaning is what is supported by complete reflection and sound consideration. For man, in the journey of his life, toward whatever goal it extends, has no concern in reality except the good of his soul and the happiness of his life, even if he is outwardly occupied with some things whose benefit returns to others. The Exalted said: **إِنْ أَحْسَنْتُمْ أَحْسَنْتُمْ لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ وَإِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَهَا** — "If you do good, you do good to your own souls, and if you do evil, it is to them [likewise]" (17:7).

There is nothing there except this human being who develops stage after stage and undergoes state after state—from embryo, to child, to youth, to middle-aged, to old man—then he who continues life in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — then on the Day of Resurrection, then what follows it of Paradise or Fire. This is the distance that man traverses from his position at the beginning of his formation until he ends at his Lord. The Exalted said: **وَأَنَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهَىٰ** — "And that the terminus is toward your Lord" (53:42).

It is man who treads no ground in his journey, nor travels by day or night, except through heart-actions—which are beliefs and the like—and limb-actions, whether righteous or wicked. What his deed produces one day becomes his provision for the next. Thus, the soul is man's path to his Lord, and Allah, the Glorified, is his Goal in his journey.

This is a compulsory path from which man has no escape, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسَانُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَاقِيهِ** — "O man! You are laboring toward your Lord with [great] labor, and you will encounter Him" (84:6). This is a path of necessary treading in which the believer and the disbeliever, the attentive and the heedless, all participate. The verse does not intend to exhort toward its treading in the sense of urging its journeying upon one who does not tread it.

Rather, the verse intends to alert the believers to this reality after their heedlessness of it. For this reality, like all other ontological realities (الحقائق التكوينية), although it is fixed and unchanging by knowledge or ignorance, man's attention to it has a prominent influence on his actions. Actions are what cultivate the human soul in a manner suitable to its essence. If the action is in harmony with the reality of the matter and suitable to the purpose of creation and origination, the soul perfected by it will be happy in its endeavor, not disappointed in its striving, nor losing in its transaction. The explanation of this has already passed in many places in this book such that no doubt remains.

To clarify this in a manner suitable to this context: man, like other creations of Allah, the Glorified, falls under divine cultivation (التربية الإلهية) without anything of his affair escaping Him. The Exalted has said: **مَا مِنْ دَابَّةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ آخِذٌ بِنَاصِيَتِهَا إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** — "There is no moving creature but He holds it by its forelock. Indeed my Lord is on a straight path" (11:56). This is an ontological cultivation (تربية تكوينية) similar to how Allah, the Glorified, cultivates other things in their collective journey toward Him, the Exalted. He said: **أَلَا إِلَى اللَّهِ تَصِيرُ الْأُمُورُ** — "Behold! To Allah do all matters come" (42:53). The matter does not vary, nor does the state differ in this cultivation between one thing and another, for the path is straight and the command is uniform and consistent. The Exalted also said: **مَا تَرَىٰ فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ مِنْ تَفَاوُتٍ** — "You do not see any discordance in the creation of the All-beneficent" (67:3).

He, the Glorified, has made the goal of man and that at which his affair ends and his outcome settles—in terms of happiness, wretchedness, success, and disappointment—built upon psychological states and traits (أحوال وأخلاق نفسانية), which are built upon actions from man. Those actions are divided into righteous and wicked, and godwariness and profligacy, as the Exalted said: **وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّاهَا * فَأَلْهَمَهَا فُجُورَهَا وَتَقْوَاهَا * قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاهَا * وَقَدْ خَابَ مَنْ دَسَّاهَا** — "By the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with its vices and its virtues: felicitous is he who purifies it, and he who ruins it is a failure" (91:7-10). These verses—as you see—place the fashioned soul on one side, which is the beginning of the state, and success and failure on the other side, which is the goal and the end of the journey. Then they build the two goals—namely success and failure—upon the purification (تزكية) of the soul and its corruption (تدسية), which is the stage of ethics (الأخلاق). Then they build virtue and vice upon godwariness (التقوى) and profligacy (الفجور)—namely righteous and wicked deeds—which the verses declare that man is inspired with from the side of Allah.

The verses, in their explanation, do not exceed the domain of the soul, in the sense that they consider the soul to be the created and fashioned entity, the one to which profligacy and godwariness are attributed, the one that is purified or corrupted, and the one in which man succeeds or fails. This, as you have known, proceeds according to the requirements of ontology (التكوين).

However, this ontological reality—namely that man in his life is journeying in the path of his own soul, unable to step away from it even by a step, nor to leave it or exit from it even for a moment—is not equal for the one who is alert to it and remembers it with a necessary remembrance that admits no forgetfulness, and the one who is heedless of it and has forgotten the reality from which there is no escape. The Exalted said: **هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ وَالَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ** — "Say, ‘Are those who know equal to those who do not know?’ Only those who possess intellects take admonition" (39:9).

And the Exalted said: **فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ * وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ * قَالَ رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِي أَعْمَىٰ وَقَدْ كُنْتُ بَصِيرًا * قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ أَتَتْكَ آيَاتُنَا فَنَسِيتَهَا وَكَذَٰلِكَ الْيَوْمَ تُنْسَىٰ** — "Then whoever follows My guidance will not go astray, nor will he be wretched. But whoever turns away from My remembrance, his life shall be a hard one, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection.’ He will say, ‘My Lord! Why have You raised me blind, though I used to see?’ He will say, ‘So it is. Our signs came to you, but you forgot them, and thus you will be forgotten today’" (20:123-126).

That is because the one alert to this reality, whenever he turns to the reality of his position relative to his Lord and his relation to the other parts of the world, finds his soul cut off from everything else—whereas he used to find it otherwise—and a veil drawn before it. Nothing touches it with encompassing and influence except its Lord, the Disposer of its affair, Who pushes it from behind and draws it forward by His power and guidance. He finds it alone with its Lord, having no guardian besides Him. At that point, he understands the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَى اللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** (To Allah is the return of you all, then He will inform you of what you used to do) after His saying: عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ لا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ (Take care of your own souls. He who strays cannot hurt you if you are guided). And the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **أَوَمَنْ كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُمَاتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِنْهَا** — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (6:122).

At that point, the perception and consciousness of the soul change. It migrates from the homeland of polytheism (الشرك) to the station of servitude (العبودية) and the rank of monotheism (التوحيد). It continues to exchange polytheism for monotheism, illusion for realization, distance for proximity, and satanic arrogance for divine humility, and illusory self-sufficiency for the poverty of servitude—if divine care takes its hand and the driver of success (التوفيق) leads it.

Although we may not be able to understand these meanings with true understanding due to our clinging to the earth and our preoccupation with the vanities of this fleeting life—which the divine speech in its explanation only identifies as play and diversion—from diving into the depths of these realities revealed by the religion and pointed to by the Divine Book. As the Exalted said: **وَمَا الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ** — "The life of this world is nothing but play and diversion" (6:32). And the Exalted said: **ذَٰلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ** — "That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge" (53:30).

However, sound consideration, exhaustive research, and sufficient reflection lead us to affirm their universals (كلياتها) in general, even if we fall short of enumerating the details. And Allah is the Guide.

Perhaps we have departed from the mode of brevity, so let us return to the beginning of the discourse and say: The verse can be taken as a social address, such that the addressee in His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (O you who have faith) is the community of believers. Thus, the meaning of His saying: عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ (Take care of your own souls) would be the believers' reform of their Islamic society by adopting the quality of being guided by divine guidance, by safeguarding their religious knowledge, righteous deeds, and general Islamic rites. As the Exalted said: **وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَلَا تَفَرَّقُوا** — "Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided" (3:103). It has already been mentioned in its commentary that the meaning of this social holding fast is to adhere to the Book and the Sunnah.

And His saying: لا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ (He who strays cannot hurt you if you are guided) would mean that they are secure from the harm of straying, non-Islamic societies. Thus, it is not obligatory upon the Muslims to go to extremes in spreading Islam among non-Muslim groups beyond the customary invitation, as previously mentioned.

Or, it means that it is not permissible for them to slip away from the guidance they possess upon witnessing the immersion of straying societies in desires and their enjoyment of the false advantages of life. For everyone's return is to Allah, and He will inform them of what they used to do. On this basis, the verse proceeds in the manner of His saying, the Exalted: **لَا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي الْبِلَادِ * مَتَاعٌ قَلِيلٌ ثُمَّ مَأْوَاهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمِهَادُ** — "Never be misled by the bustle of the faithless in the towns. It is a fleeting enjoyment; then their abode is hell, and it is an evil resting place" (3:196-197). And His saying: **وَلَا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلَىٰ مَا مَتَّعْنَا بِهِ أَزْوَاجًا مِنْهُمْ زَهْرَةَ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا** — "Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them as a glitter of the life of this world" (20:131).

There is another meaning for His saying: لا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ (He who strays cannot hurt you if you are guided) from the perspective that the harm negated in the verse is attributed to the very person of the straying ones, without specifying any particular trait or action of theirs; thus, it implies absolute negation. The meaning would be the negation of the disbelievers being able to harm the Islamic society by changing it into a non-Islamic society through coercive force. Thus, the verse would be phrased in the manner of His saying, the Exalted: **الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ فَلَا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَاخْشَوْنِ** — "Today the faithless have despaired of your religion. So do not fear them, but fear Me" (5:3). And His saying: **لَنْ يَضُرُّوكُمْ إِلَّا أَذًى وَإِنْ يُقَاتِلُوكُمْ يُوَلُّوكُمُ الْأَدْبَارَ** — "They will never do you any harm, except for some annoyance; and if they fight you, they will turn their backs [to flee]" (3:111).

A group of the exegetes of the predecessors (السلف) mentioned that the import of the verse is a permission to abandon religious invitation and enjoining the good and forbidding the evil. They mentioned that the verse is specific, restricted to a time or state in which the condition for invitation and enjoining the good and forbidding the evil—namely, security from harm—is not present. They have narrated narrations regarding this which will be pointed to in the upcoming traditional discussion.

The necessary consequence of this meaning is that His saying: لا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ (He who strays cannot hurt you if you are guided) is a metonymy for the negation of obligation—meaning, there is no obligation upon you in that regard. Otherwise, the harm to the religious society from the prevalence of straying, whether unfaith or profligacy, is something no doubter would doubt.

However, that is a remote meaning that the context of the verse does not support. For if the verse were taken as a specifier (مخصصة) for the generalities of the obligation of invitation and enjoining the good and forbidding the evil, its phrasing is not the phrasing of specification. And if it were taken as abrogating (ناسخة), then the verses of invitation and enjoining the good and forbidding the evil are resistant to abrogation. The discourse has a completion that will reach you.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Ghurar wa al-Durar* by al-Amidi, narrated from Ali (peace be upon him), he said: **من عرف نفسه عرف ربه** — "Whoever knows his soul has known his Lord."

**I say:** It has also been narrated by both groups from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and it is a famous hadith. Some scholars have mentioned that it is of the type of "suspending upon the impossible," and its import is the impossibility of knowing the soul due to the impossibility of scientific encompassment of Allah, the Glorified. This is refuted, firstly, by his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in another narration: **أعرفكم بنفسه أعرفكم بربه** — "The most knowing of you of his soul is the most knowing of you of his Lord." Secondly, the hadith is in the sense of the contrapositive (عكس النقيض) of His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَأَنْسَاهُمْ أَنْفُسَهُمْ** — "And do not be like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget their own souls" (59:19).

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **الكيس من عرف نفسه وأخلص أعماله** — "The sagacious one is he who knows his soul and makes his deeds sincere."

**I say:** The meaning of the connection of sincerity (الإخلاص) and its branching from the preoccupation with the knowledge of the soul has already passed in the preceding commentary.

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **المعرفة بالنفس أنفع المعرفتين** — "Knowledge of the soul is the more beneficial of the two knowledges."

**I say:** It appears that what is meant by the "two knowledges" is the knowledge of the internal signs (الآيات الأنفسية) and the knowledge of the external signs (الآيات الآفاقية). The Exalted said: **سَنُرِيهِمْ آيَاتِنَا فِي الْآفَاقِ وَفِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُّ أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ** — "Soon We shall show them Our signs in the horizons and in their own souls until it becomes clear to them that He is the Real. Is it not sufficient that your Lord is witness to all things?" (41:53). And the Exalted said: **وَفِي الْأَرْضِ آيَاتٌ لِلْمُوقِنِينَ * وَفِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ** — "In the earth are signs for those who have certainty, and in your own souls [as well]. Will you not then perceive?" (51:20-21).

The fact that the internal journey (السير الأنفسي) is more beneficial than the external journey (السير الآفاقي) is perhaps because psychological knowledge is usually inseparable from the reform of its traits and actions, unlike external knowledge. This is because the benefit of knowing the signs lies in the fact that knowing the signs qua signs leads to the knowledge of Allah, the Glorified, and His names, attributes, and acts—such as His being, the Exalted, Living and not subject to death, Powerful and not mixed with incapacity, Knowing and not mingled with ignorance; and that He, the Exalted, is the Creator of everything, the Owner of everything, and the Lord Sustainer over every soul for what it has earned. He created the creation not out of a need for them, but to bestow grace upon them for what they deserved, then He will gather them for the Day of Gathering, about which there is no doubt, to recompense those who did evil for what they did and to recompense those who did good with the best.

These and similar ones are true knowledges which, if man takes them up and masters them, represent to him the reality of his life: that it is an eternal life possessed of permanent happiness or necessary wretchedness, and not that impulsive, disconnected, playful, and vain life. This is a scientific position that guides man to duties and functions relative to his Lord and relative to his fellow humans in the life of this world and the life of the hereafter. These are what we call religion (الدين). For the way (السنة) that man adheres to in his life—and from which even the bedouin and the barbarian are not free—he only establishes and adheres to it, or takes and adheres to it for himself, insofar as he posits for himself a type of life, whatever type it may be. Then he acts according to what he deems good of the way to make that life happy. This is of the utmost clarity.

Thus, the life that man posits for himself represents to him the needs suitable to it; he is then guided by them to the actions that usually ensure the fulfillment of those needs. Man then applies his action to them, and that is the way (السنة) or the religion (الدين).

It follows from what we have mentioned that looking into the internal and external signs and knowing Allah, the Glorified, through them guides man to hold fast to the true religion and the divine law, because the aforementioned knowledge represents eternal human life to him at that point, and its connection to monotheism (التوحيد), the Return (المعاد), and Prophethood (النبوة).

This is a guidance toward faith and godwariness in which both paths participate together—namely, the paths of looking at the horizons and the souls. They are both beneficial, except that looking at the signs of the soul is more beneficial, for it is not devoid of discovering the essence of the soul, its powers, its spiritual and physical instruments, and what occurs to it of moderation in its affair, or its transgression, or its dormancy, and the virtuous or base traits (الملكات), and the good or bad states that accompany it.

Man's preoccupation with knowing these matters and affirming what they entail of security or danger, and happiness or wretchedness, is inseparable from his knowing the disease and the cure from a close position. He then occupies himself with reforming the corrupt among them and adhering to the sound among them. This is unlike looking at the external signs, for although it calls toward the reform of the soul and its purification from the trivialities and vices of character, and its adornment with spiritual virtues, it calls for that from a distant place, and this is obvious.

The narration has another, more subtle meaning extracted from the results of true research in psychology: looking at the external signs and the knowledge resulting from that is a conceptual looking (نظر فكري) and acquired knowledge (علم حصولي), unlike looking at the soul, its powers, and the stages of its existence, and the knowledge that manifests from it, for that is a visionary looking (نظر شهودي) and knowledge by presence (علم حضوري). Conceptual affirmation requires for its realization the ordering of syllogisms and the use of demonstration; it remains as long as man is attentive to its premises, not heedless of them nor occupied with something else. Therefore, knowledge vanishes with the vanishing of the oversight of its proof, and doubts multiply within it, and disagreement erupts in it.

This is unlike the psychological knowledge of the soul, its powers, and the stages of its existence, for it is of the nature of direct vision (العيان). When man occupies himself with looking at the signs of his soul and witnesses its poverty toward its Lord and its need in all stages of its existence, he finds a wondrous matter: he finds his soul attached to Greatness and Majesty, connected in its existence, life, knowledge, power, hearing, sight, will, love, and all its other attributes and acts to that which has no end of splendor, brilliance, beauty, glory, and perfection of existence, life, knowledge, power, and every other perfection.

He witnesses what has been previously explained: that the human soul has no affair except within itself, no exit for it from itself, and no occupation for it except the compulsory journey in the path of itself. He finds that it is cut off from everything it thought it was gathered with or mixed with, except its Lord Who encompasses its interior and exterior and everything besides it. Thus, it finds that it is always in solitude (خلاء) with its Lord, even if it is in a multitude (ملاء) of people.

At that point, it turns away from everything and turns toward its Lord; it forgets everything and remembers its Lord. No veil hides Him from it, nor does it hide from Him with any covering. This is the true knowledge (حق المعرفة) that has been decreed for man.

This knowledge is more worthy of being called "knowing Allah by Allah" (معرفة الله بالله). As for the conceptual knowledge provided by looking at the external signs—whether obtained from syllogism, intuition, or otherwise—it is only knowledge of a mental form (صورة ذهنية) about a mental form. Far exalted is the Divinity that a mind should encompass Him, or that His essence should be equal to a fabricated form fabricated by a creation of His creation; "and they do not encompass Him in knowledge."

It has been narrated in *al-Irshad* and *al-Ihtijaj*, as found in *al-Bihar*, from al-Sha'bi from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) in a discourse of his: "Indeed, Allah is too majestic to be veiled from anything or for anything to be veiled from Him." In *al-Tawhid*, from Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him) in a discourse of his: "There is no veil between Him and His creation except His creation—He is veiled without a hidden veil, and concealed without a covered covering; there is no god but He, the Great, the Most Exalted." In *al-Tawhid*, with a chain of transmission from Abd al-A'la from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a hadith: "Whoever claims that he knows Allah by a veil, or a form, or a likeness—he is a polytheist, because the veil, the form, and the likeness are other than Him. He is only One, Unified; so how can he be unified by one who claims he unifies Him by other than Him? Only he has known Allah who has known Him by Allah. So whoever does not know Him by Him—he does not know Him; he only knows something else," the hadith. The reports transmitted from the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them) regarding the meaning of what we have presented are very many; perhaps Allah will grant us success to cite and explain them in what will follow, Allah the Mighty willing, of the commentary of Surah al-A'raf.

It has thus been established that looking at the signs of the souls is more precious and of higher value, and that it alone is what produces the reality of knowledge. Accordingly, his (peace be upon him) counting it as the "more beneficial of the two knowledges," rather than the only knowledge, is because the generality of people are incapable of attaining it. The Book and the Sunnah have concurred, and the pure Prophetic biography and the biography of the pure People of his House have proceeded upon accepting the one who believes in Allah through external looking, which is the common looking among the believers. Thus, both paths are beneficial, but the benefit in the path of the soul is more complete and more abundant.

In *al-Durar wa al-Ghurar*, from Ali (peace be upon him), he said: **العارف من عرف نفسه فأعتقها ونزهها عن كل ما يبعدها** — "The gnostic is he who knows his soul, thus he frees it and purifies it from everything that distances it."

**I say:** Meaning, he frees it from the captivity of passion and the slavery of desires.

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **أعظم الجهل جهل الإنسان أمر نفسه** — "The greatest ignorance is man's ignorance of the affair of his soul."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **أعظم الحكمة معرفة الإنسان نفسه** — "The greatest wisdom is man's knowledge of his soul."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **أكثر الناس معرفة لنفسه أخوفهم لربه** — "The most knowing of people of his soul is the most fearful of them of his Lord."

**I say:** That is because he is the most knowing of them of his Lord and the most cognizant of Him. Allah, the Glorified, has said: **إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى اللَّهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ الْعُلَمَاءُ** — "Only those of Allah’s servants who possess knowledge fear Him" (35:28).

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **أفضل العقل معرفة المرء بنفسه فمن عرف نفسه عقل ، ومن جهلها ضل** — "The best of intellect is a man's knowledge of his soul; so whoever knows his soul has intellect, and whoever is ignorant of it has strayed."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **عجبت لمن ينشد ضالته ، وقد أضل نفسه فلا يطلبها** — "I wonder at the one who seeks his lost property, while he has lost his soul and does not seek it."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **عجبت لمن يجهل نفسه كيف يعرف ربه؟** — "I wonder at the one who is ignorant of his soul, how can he know his Lord?"

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **غاية المعرفة أن يعرف المرء نفسه** — "The ultimate reach of knowledge is for a man to know his soul."

**I say:** The reason for it being the ultimate reach of knowledge has already passed, for it is the reality of knowledge.

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **كيف يعرف غيره من يجهل نفسه** — "How can he know another who is ignorant of his soul?"

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **كفى بالمرء معرفة أن يعرف نفسه ، وكفى بالمرء جهلا أن يجهل نفسه** — "It is sufficient knowledge for a man that he knows his soul, and it is sufficient ignorance for a man that he is ignorant of his soul."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **من عرف نفسه تجرد** — "Whoever knows his soul becomes detached."

**I say:** Meaning, he becomes detached from the attachments of the world, or detached from people by withdrawing from them, or detached from everything through sincerity to Allah.

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **من عرف نفسه جاهدها ومن جهل نفسه أهملها** — "Whoever knows his soul struggles against it, and whoever is ignorant of his soul neglects it."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **من عرف نفسه جل أمره** — "Whoever knows his soul, his affair becomes great."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **من عرف نفسه كان لغيره أعرف ـ ومن جهل نفسه كان بغيره أجهل** — "Whoever knows his soul will be more knowing of others, and whoever is ignorant of his soul will be more ignorant of others."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **من عرف نفسه فقد انتهى إلى غاية كل معرفة وعلم** — "Whoever knows his soul has reached the ultimate end of every knowledge and science."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **من لم يعرف نفسه بعد عن سبيل النجاة ، وخبط في الضلال والجهالات** — "Whoever does not know his soul has distanced himself from the path of salvation and stumbled in straying and ignorances."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **معرفة النفس أنفع المعارف** — "Knowledge of the soul is the most beneficial of knowledges."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **نال الفوز الأكبر من ظفر بمعرفة النفس** — "He has attained the greatest victory who has triumphed in the knowledge of the soul."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him), he said: **لا تجهل نفسك فإن الجاهل معرفة نفسه جاهل بكل شيء** — "Do not be ignorant of your soul, for the one ignorant of the knowledge of his soul is ignorant of everything."

In *Tuhaf al-Uqul*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a hadith: "Whoever claims that he knows Allah by the imagination of hearts is a polytheist. Whoever claims that he knows Allah by the name without the meaning—he has admitted to defamation because the name is originated (محدث). Whoever claims that he worships the name and the meaning—he has set up a partner with Allah. Whoever claims that he worships by the attribute without perception—he has referred to an absent one. Whoever claims that he adds the described to the attribute—he has belittled the Great; and they have not esteemed Allah with the esteem due to Him."

He was asked: "Then what is the way of monotheism (التوحيد)?" He said: "The door of research is possible and the seeking of an exit exists—indeed, the knowledge of the essence of the Witness (عين الشاهد) is before His attribute, and the knowledge of the attribute of the Absent (الغائب) is before his essence."

It was said: "And how is the essence of the Witness known before His attribute?" He said: "You know Him and learn His knowledge, and you know yourself by Him and you do not know yourself from yourself. You learn that what is in it is for Him and by Him, just as they said to Joseph: **إِنَّكَ لَأَنْتَ يُوسُفُ** — 'Are you indeed Joseph?' (12:90). He said: **أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهَٰذَا أَخِي** — 'I am Joseph, and this is my brother.' Thus they knew him by him and did not know him by another, nor did they establish him from themselves by the imagination of hearts," the hadith.

**I say:** We have clarified under his (peace be upon him) saying: "Knowledge of the soul is the more beneficial of the two knowledges" (the second narration of the chapter) that when man occupies himself with the sign of his soul and is alone with it apart from others, he becomes cut off toward his Lord from everything. This is followed by the knowledge of his Lord—a knowledge without the mediation of a medium, and a science without the causation of a cause. For the cutting off (الانقطاع) removes every drawn veil. At that point, man becomes oblivious of himself by witnessing the courtyard of Greatness and Majesty. This knowledge is more worthy of being called "knowing Allah by Allah."

It is revealed to him at that point from the reality of his soul that it is the one in poverty toward Allah, the Glorified, owned by Him with an ownership that does not independently possess anything besides Him. This is the meaning of his (peace be upon him) saying: "You know yourself by Him, and you do not know yourself by yourself from yourself, and you learn that what is in it is for Him and by Him."

In this meaning is what al-Mas'udi narrated in *Ithbat al-Wasiyyah* from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said in a sermon of his: "So glory be to You! You have filled everything and are distinct from everything—thus nothing misses You, and You are the Doer of what You will. Blessed are You, O You from Whom every perceived thing is of His creation, and every limited thing is of His making."

—until he said— "Glory be to You! What eye can stand before the splendor of Your light and ascend to the light of the brilliance of Your power? And what understanding can understand what is below that, except eyes from which the coverings have been removed and from which the dark veils have been torn? Their spirits ascended on the tips of the wings of spirits, so they communed with You in Your pillars, and entered between the lights of Your splendor, and looked from the vantage point of the dust to the level of Your majesty. So the people of the Kingdom (الملكوت) called them visitors, and the people of the Omnipotence (الجبروت) called them inhabitants."

In *al-Bihar*, from *Irshad al-Daylami*—and he mentioned after that two chains of transmission for this hadith—and in it: "Whoever acts for My pleasure, I shall make three traits necessary for him: I shall make him know a gratitude not mixed with ignorance, a remembrance not mixed with forgetfulness, and a love such that he does not prefer the love of creatures over My love.

So when he loves Me, I love him, and I open the eye of his heart to My majesty, and I do not hide from him the elite of My creation. I commune with him in the darkness of the night and the light of the day—until his conversation with the creatures and his sitting with them is cut off. I make him hear My speech and the speech of My angels, and I make him know the secret that I have hidden from My creation. I clothe him in modesty until all creation is modest before him. He walks upon the earth forgiven, and I make his heart attentive and seeing. I do not hide from him anything of Paradise or Fire, and I make him know what people undergo on the Day of Resurrection of terror and hardship, and how I reckon the rich and the poor—the ignorant and the learned. I make him sleep in his grave and I send down upon him Munkar and Nakir until they question him. He does not see the grief of death, the darkness of the grave and the niche, nor the terror of the place of oversight. Then I set up for him his balance and spread out his register, then I place his book in his right hand and he reads it spread out—then I do not place between Me and him an interpreter. These are the attributes of the lovers.

O Ahmad! Make your concern a single concern, and make your tongue a single tongue, and make your body alive, never heedless. Whoever is heedless of Me, I do not care in which valley he perishes."

The last three narrations, although they are not among the reports of this research established upon the straight path, we have only cited them so that the insightful critic may judge by what we have presented: that true knowledge is not fully satisfied by conceptual science (العلم الفكري). For the narrations mention matters of divine gifts specific to His friends (أولياؤه) that the conceptual journey does not produce at all.

These are straight and sound reports whose soundness is witnessed by the Divine Book, as we shall clarify in what will reach you of the commentary of Surah al-A'raf, Allah the Mighty willing.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ (the verse), he said: He (peace be upon him) said: "Reform your own souls—and do not follow the faults of people—and do not mention them, for their straying does not hurt you if you are righteous."

**I say:** The narration is consistent with what we presented in the preceding commentary: that the verse is directed toward the prohibition of interfering in the reform of people's state beyond the customary invitation and enjoining the good and forbidding the evil. It is not phrased as a permission to abandon the obligation of invitation and enjoining the good and forbidding the evil.

In *Nahj al-Bayan*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), that he said: "This verse was revealed regarding dissimulation (التقية)."

**I say:** The import of the narration is that the verse is specific to the case of dissimulation (التقية) — *the practice of concealing one's faith in the face of danger* — from the people of straying regarding the invitation to the truth and enjoining the good and forbidding the evil, due to the legal condition of that being the absence of dissimulation. We have already mentioned in the preceding commentary that the literal meaning of the verse does not support this.

It has been narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from the exegetes of the predecessors (السلف) the saying of a group of them to that effect, such as Ibn Mas'ud, Ibn Umar, Ubayy ibn Ka'b, Ibn Abbas, and Makhul. The narrations reported regarding this from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) do not indicate that.

They are what is from al-Tirmidhi, who graded it sound, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, al-Baghawi in his *Mu'jam*, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Hakim, who graded it sound, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Shu'ab*, from Abu Umayyah al-Sha'bani, who said: "I came to Abu Tha'labah al-Khushani and said to him: 'How do you deal with this verse?' He said: 'Which verse?' I said: His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ. He said: 'By Allah, you have asked one well-informed about it. I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about it. He said: "Rather, enjoin the good and forbid the evil—until you see obeyed greed, followed passion, a preferred world, and every possessor of an opinion being amazed by his own opinion—then take care of your own soul and leave the affair of the commoners. For behind you are days of patience; the one patient in them is like one grasping a hot coal. For the worker in them is the like of the reward of fifty men who work the like of your work."'"

**I say:** In this meaning is what Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Mu'adh ibn Jabal from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The narration only indicates that enjoining the good and forbidding the evil were not removed by the verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ahmad, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Amir al-Ash'ari: that there was something among them, so he was delayed from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Then he came to him and he said: "What delayed you?" He said: "O Messenger of Allah! I read this verse: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ." He said: The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to him: "Where have you gone? It is only: 'He who strays from the disbelievers cannot hurt you if you are guided.'"

**I say:** The narration, as you see, restricts the matter in the verse to the permission to abandon the invitation of the disbelievers to the truth, and diverts it from the permission to abandon enjoining the good and forbidding the evil in the branches (الفروع). This is despite the fact that the verses of the obligation of invitation and what follows it of the verses of jihad and the like are no less resistant to that than the verses of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil.

In the same source: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "This verse was mentioned in the presence of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ. The Prophet of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Its interpretation (تأويلها) has not come; its interpretation will not come until Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him) descends.'"

**I say:** The discussion regarding this narration is similar to the discussion regarding what preceded.

In the same source: Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Hudhayfah: regarding His saying: عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ لا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ. He said: "When you have enjoined the good and forbidden the evil."

**I say:** This is a balanced meaning whose outcome returns to what we mentioned. The like of it was narrated from Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib.

## (Scientific Research)

Composed of historical indications and other psychological researches and so forth, in chapters:

1 - Man has not ceased, as far as we know—even the primitive man—to say in some of his speech: "I" (أنا) and "my soul" (نفسي). He refers by it to a reality among the ontological realities (الحقائق الكونية). He undoubtedly knows what he says and understands what he intends. However, the turning of his concern toward the mobilization of the pillars of physical life and his preoccupation with bodily actions to fulfill material needs diverts him from deepening his understanding of the affair of this soul referred to by his saying: "I" and "my soul." Perhaps this has cast into his imagination that it is the body and nothing else.

Perhaps man found that the distinction between the living and the dead according to the appearance of the senses is the breath (النفس) by which man breathes as long as he is alive. When he loses it or its passages are blocked, he becomes dead, perceiving nothing; his existence is nullified and his personality and identity (إنيته) are annihilated. Thus, he affirmed that the soul (النفس) is the breath (النَّفَس), which is wind or a specific type of wind; therefore, he called it spirit (الروح), and judged that man is the composite of the spirit and the body.

Or, he saw that bodily sensation and movement are as if they were hostages to what is trapped in the body of the blood circulating in its limbs or flowing in its vessels of arteries and veins, and that the life whose departure is the departure of humanity is connected to this red fluid in existence and non-existence. Thus, he judged that the soul is the blood; he called the soul blood, rather, the blood a "flowing soul" (نفساً سائلة) or non-flowing.

Perhaps what man witnesses of the affair of the sperm—that the semen, when the womb swallows it and ontological development (التطور الكوني) occurs to it stage after stage, is what becomes a human—called him to go toward the view that the human soul is the original parts gathered in the sperm. These remain in the bodily structure throughout life. Perhaps some went to the view that they are protected from change and nullification, and that humanity remains by their remaining, untouched by the hand of events, nor do they accept nullification and annihilation. This is despite the fact that if the human soul were these described parts—whether we stipulate their gathering in a specific form or do not stipulate it—it would entail many impossible consequences mentioned in their proper place.

These sayings and their likes do not contradict what man attains, being a human, of the reality of his saying: "I" and "my soul." He does not err in it at all. For it is not far-fetched that we perceive one of the ontological realities in general with an unerring perception, then we begin to research its identity (هويته) and the reality of its affair in detail, and thus we err in it at that point. There are many scientific subjects, such as the external or internal sensibles, which we witness with direct vision—despite the Sophists and the skeptics—yet scholars have not ceased to differ regarding their affair, generation after generation.

Likewise, the commoners among those who are not people of research witness from themselves what the elite witness, without any difference at all. Yet they are in ignorance of the affair of its detail, incapable of explaining the specificities of its existence.

In short, there is no doubt that man, in all times of his existence, witnesses an affair not external to him, which he expresses by "I" and "my soul." If his vision becomes subtle and he deepens his immersion in what he finds in this witnessing of his, he finds it to be something contrary to what he finds of bodily matters that are susceptible to change, division, and association with place and time. He finds it to be other than this material body governed by the laws of matter with its limbs and parts. For he may forget any limb of his limbs or be heedless of his entire body, yet he does not forget his soul nor is he heedless of it. Leave aside what you may say: "I forgot myself," "I was heedless of myself," "I was oblivious of myself"—for these are metaphors for various psychological attentions. Do you not see that you attribute the forgetting, heedlessness, and obliviousness then to your soul, and you judge that your perceiving soul perceived a matter and was heedless of a matter you call your soul, such as the body and the like?

Leave aside the illusion that a person in a state of unconsciousness (المغمى عليه) is heedless of his essence and his self. For what such a person finds after the state of unconsciousness has passed is that he does not remember his consciousness of self during that state, not that he remembers being unconscious of it; there is a profound difference between these two meanings. Furthermore, some who have been unconscious may remember something from that state resembling the visions we recall from the state of sleep.

In any case, there should be no doubt that a human being, insofar as he is a human being, is never devoid of this self-awareness (الشعور النفسي) which represents to him the reality of his self, expressed by the word "I." If one were to become even slightly acquainted with what he observes of himself—by turning away from being divided among bodily preoccupations and material desires—he would conclude, based on what has preceded, that his self is an entity distinct from the nature of matter and material things (الماديات), as he observes the distinctness of the properties and effects of his self from the properties and effects of material matters.

However, preoccupation with daily concerns, the directing of ambition toward the desires of material life, and the fulfillment of bodily needs call him to neglect this matter, to submit to some of those naive, rudimentary opinions, and to stop at the mere generality of observation.

2 — Although the average individual is preoccupied with concerns of food, shelter, clothing, and marriage—distracting him from delving into the reality of his self and searching the corners of his essence—the various events that assault him during the days of his life are rarely devoid of factors that direct him to turn away from others and be alone with his self. Examples include intense fear, by which the soul is disturbed by everything and returns to itself, as if seizing and holding onto it for fear of annihilation (الفناء) and cessation (الزوال); or joy and grief, which cause the soul to be drawn toward what it finds pleasurable; or intense passion, leading to infatuation with the beloved sought, such that no concern remains but that of the beloved; or extreme necessity (الاضطرار), by which a person is cut off from everything and returns to his self; among other incidental factors.

These various factors and diverse causes may lead a person—through one or more of them—to have represented before him things that the external senses (الحواس الظاهرة) or empty thought can hardly attain. For instance, one who finds himself in a dark, desolate place, overwhelmed by fear for his life, may see terrifying things or hear horrific voices threatening him; this is what people sometimes call a ghoul (غول), a calling voice (هاتف), a jinn (جن), and the like.

Sometimes, intense love or profound longing and regret may surround him, coming between him and his external senses and concentrating his consciousness on what he loves or regrets. Consequently, he sees—either in a state of sleep or in a state of wakefulness resembling sleep—various matters from past events, future incidents, or hidden secrets (خبايا وخفايا) that are concealed from the senses of others.

Furthermore, when the will (الإرادة) is joined by certainty (اليقين), intense faith (الإيمان), and firm conviction (الإذعان الجازم), it may perform acts that an ordinary human being is incapable of, and which normal causes (الأسباب العادية) cannot lead to.

These are rare, particular events (حوادث جزئية) — relative to the generality of ordinary events — that occur due to the emergence of the various factors previously mentioned. As for the fact of their occurrence, there is little need to labor over proving it, for none of us is without a memory of himself or others that testifies to it. As for what the true cause (السبب الحقيقي) acting within them is, this is not the place to engage with that.

What is important for us to notice is that all these matters depend for their occurrence on a kind of turning away of the soul from preoccupation with things external to it — especially physical pleasures — and its turning back upon itself. Therefore, the foundation of all psychological disciplines (الارتياضات النفسانية) — in all their countless varieties and diversities — is the opposition to the soul (مخالفة النفس) in general. This is only because the soul’s devotion to following its whims (هواها) diverts it from preoccupation with itself and directs it toward its external desires; it thus distributes the soul among them and divides its consciousness between them, so the soul takes hold of them and abandons itself.

3 — We should not doubt that the factors inviting these psychological effects, just as they are realized for some individuals temporarily and in brief moments, may be realized for others as stable and continuous, or may last for a significant duration. We often find individuals who are ascetic (متزهدين) toward the world and its material pleasures and vanishing desires, having no concern but the training of the soul (ترويض النفس) and engaging in the journey of the inner path (سلوك طريق الباطن).

Nor should we doubt that this psychological preoccupation is not an innovated practice (سنة مبتدعة) in our time. Both transmission (النقل) and rational consideration (الاعتبار) indicate that it was among the practices circulating among people; the further back we go, we find it among the necessary practices of humanity since its earliest eras on this earth, as we reckon.

4 — Researching the state of nations, contemplating their customs and biographies, and analyzing their beliefs and actions reveals that engaging in self-knowledge (معرفة النفس) through its various methods to obtain its wondrous effects was prevalent among them. Indeed, it was a precious endeavor for which the most valuable time and the highest prices were spent since the most ancient ages.

Evidence for this is found in the primitive peoples living on the fringes of the inhabited world, such as in Africa and elsewhere, where remnants of myths of sorcery (السحر) and divination (الكهانة) and the belief in their reality and efficacy exist to this day.

Careful consideration of what has been transmitted to us of ancient doctrines and religions—such as Brahmanism, Buddhism, Sabaeanism, Manichaeism, Zoroastrianism, Judaism, Christianity, and Islam—all indicates that the important task was self-knowledge and obtaining its effects by delving deeply into it, even if they differed in their description, indoctrination, and evaluation.

Brahmanism — which is the doctrine of ancient India — although it differs from the scriptural religions regarding monotheism (التوحيد) and the matter of prophethood (النبوة), nevertheless calls for the purification of the soul (تزكية النفس) and the cleansing of the inner secret (تطهير السر), especially for the Brahmins themselves.

It is narrated from al-Biruni in his book *What is of India* (ما للهند من مقولة) that he said: "The life of a Brahmin after seven years have passed is divided into four parts:

The first part begins in the eighth year, when the Brahmins gather to awaken him, inform him of his duties, and enjoin him to adhere to and embrace them as long as he lives."

He said: "He enters the first part from the eighth year until the twenty-fifth year of his age, and then until the forty-eighth year. In this period, it is mandatory for him to be ascetic, to make the earth his bed, and to devote himself to learning the 'Veda' (بيذ) — its interpretation being the science of theology and law — from a teacher whom he serves day and night. He must bathe three times every day, offer the fire sacrifice at both ends of the day, prostrate to his teacher after the sacrifice, fast one day and break his fast the next while abstaining from meat entirely. His residence is in the teacher's house, and he leaves it to beg and solicit from only five houses once a day at noon or evening. Whatever charity he finds, he places before his teacher so that he may choose what he wants, then he permits him the remainder, so he nourishes himself with what is left. He carries the firewood to the fire, for the fire is venerated among them and the lights are considered close [to the divine]."

Likewise, among other nations, they used to see the acceptance of a sacrifice by the descent of fire upon it, and they were not deterred from this by the worship of idols, planets, cows, donkeys, or images.

He said: "As for the second part, it is from the twenty-fifth year to the fiftieth or seventieth. In this part, the teacher permits him to marry, so he weds and seeks progeny." He then mentioned the manner of his interaction with his family and people, his livelihood, and his conduct.

Then he said: "As for the third part, it is from the fiftieth to the seventy-fifth or ninetieth. In this part, he becomes an ascetic, leaves the luxuries of life, and hands over his wife to his children if she does not accompany him to the wilderness. He continues outside of civilization following his conduct in the first part; he does not take shelter under a roof, wears only what covers his private parts from the bark of trees, sleeps only on the ground without a bed, nourishes himself only with fruits, plants, and their roots, lets his hair grow long, and does not use oil."

He said: "As for the fourth part, it is until the end of his life. In it, he wears red clothing, takes a staff in his hand, and devotes himself to thought and stripping the heart (تجريد القلب) of friendships and enmities. He rejects desire, greed, and anger, and keeps no company whatsoever.

If he intends to go to a virtuous place seeking reward, he does not stay on his way in a village for more than one day, nor in a city for more than five days. If someone gives him something, he does not leave any of it for the next day. He has nothing but to persevere in the conditions of the path leading to salvation (الخلاص) and reaching the station from which there is no return to the world." He then mentioned the general laws that a Brahmin must act upon throughout his life. This is the end of the relevant portion of his words.

As for the other religious sects of the Indians, such as the Yogis (الجوكية) — the masters of breaths and imaginations — and the masters of spiritualities, the masters of wisdom, and others, each group among them has arduous practical disciplines (رياضات شاقة) that are not devoid of seclusion and the prohibition of sensual pleasures for the soul.

As for Buddhism, the foundation of their doctrine is the refinement of the soul (تهذيب النفس), opposing its whims, and prohibiting its pleasures to obtain the reality of knowledge (حقيقة المعرفة). This was the path followed by Buddha himself in his life. It is narrated that he was of the sons of kings or leaders, but he rejected the luxuries of life and abandoned the throne for a desolate forest which he inhabited in the prime of his youth. He secluded himself from people, left the enjoyment of life's advantages, and turned toward the training of his soul and contemplating the secrets of creation until knowledge was cast into his heart, his age then being thirty-six. At that point, he went out to the people and called them to the training of the soul and the acquisition of knowledge, and he continued in that for nearly forty-four years, according to history.

As for the Sabaeans — and we mean by them the masters of spiritualities and their idols — although they denied the matter of prophethood, they have methods for reaching the perfection of self-knowledge (كمال المعرفة النفسانية) that do not differ much from the methods of the Brahmins and Buddhists. They said — as recorded in *al-Milal wa al-Nihal*: "It is obligatory for us to purify our souls from the filth of natural desires and refine our character from the attachments of the appetitive and irascible powers, until a correspondence occurs between us and the spiritual beings (الروحانيات). Then we ask our needs from them, present our states to them, and yearn toward them in all our affairs. They then intercede for us with our Creator and theirs, our Provider and theirs. This purification is only achieved through our acquisition, our discipline, and our weaning of our souls from base desires, seeking aid from the direction of the spiritual beings. Seeking aid consists of supplication and entreaty through prayers, performing devotions, giving alms, fasting from food and drink, offering sacrifices and slaughters, burning incense, and reciting incantations. Thus, our souls obtain readiness and aid without a mediator." End quote.

These people, although they differed among themselves somewhat in general beliefs regarding creation and origination, were in agreement regarding the necessity of training the soul to obtain the perfection of knowledge and the happiness of the [next] state of existence.

As for the Manichaeans among the dualists (الثنوية), the establishment of their doctrine is well known: that the soul is from the celestial world of light and descended into these dark material nets called bodies, and that its happiness and perfection lie in escaping from the abode of darkness to the courtyard of light, either voluntarily through psychological discipline or by necessity through natural death.

As for the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — and we mean by them the Jews, Christians, and Zoroastrians — their sacred books, namely the Old Testament, the New Testament, and the Avesta, are filled with the call to reform the soul, refine it, and oppose its whims.

The books of the two Testaments continue to mention asceticism in the world and engaging in the purification of the inner secret. A large number of ascetics and those who abandon the world continue to be raised among them generation after generation, especially the Christians, for monasticism (الرهبانية) is among their followed practices.

The matter of their monasticism is mentioned in the Noble Qur’an. The Exalted says: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيسِينَ وَرُهْباناً وَأَنَّهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ** — "That is because there are priests and monks among them, and because they are not arrogant" (al-Ma'idah: 82). And the Exalted says: **وَرَهْبانِيَّةً ابتدعوها ما كتبناها عليهم إلا ابتغاء رضوان الله فما رعوها حق رعايتها** — "And as for monasticism, they innovated it—We did not prescribe it for them—only seeking Allah’s pleasure. Yet they did not observe it with due care" (al-Hadid: 27). Likewise, the worshippers among the Jews are mentioned in His saying: **لَيْسُوا سَواءً مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ أُمَّةٌ قائِمَةٌ يَتْلُونَ آياتِ اللهِ آناءَ اللَّيْلِ وَهُمْ يَسْجُدُونَ ، يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ وَيُسارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْراتِ وَأُولئِكَ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** — "They are not all alike. Among the People of the Book is an upright community: they recite Allah’s signs in the watches of the night and prostrate. They believe in Allah and the Last Day, and bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and are active in [performing] good deeds. Those are among the righteous" (Al 'Imran: 113-114).

As for the various groups of practitioners of psychological disciplines and acts, such as the masters of sorcery (السحر), alchemy (السيمياء), talismans (الطلسمات), the subjugation of spirits, jinn, and the spiritualities of letters and planets, and the masters of summoning and the subjugation of souls — each of them has specific psychological disciplines that result in a kind of authority over the matter of the soul.

The summary of the matter, as derived from all that has passed, is that the ultimate direction for all adherents of religions, doctrines, and practices is the refinement of the soul by abandoning its whims and engaging in its purification from the mixture of characters and states that are unsuitable for the objective.

5 — You might return and say: "What has been established from the practices and biographies of the adherents of doctrines and paths is asceticism in the world, which is different from the issue of self-knowledge (معرفة النفس) or engaging with the matter of the soul in the sense previously discussed."

In clearer terms: "That which religions and doctrines call for — those that invite to servitude (العبودية) such that a person practices a kind of asceticism in the world by performing righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) and abandoning whims, sins, and base character traits — is to prepare thereby for the best reward, either in the Hereafter, as explicitly stated by the prophetic religions like Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, or in this world, as established in the religion of idolatry, the doctrine of transmigration (التناسخ), and others."

"The worshipper, according to the religious instruction, performs what he is invited to of the type of asceticism without it occurring to his mind that there is an immaterial soul (نفس مجردة) and that it has a kind of knowledge in which lies its happiness and the perfection of its existence."

"Likewise, the practitioner of disciplines, despite the diversity of their paths and practices, only undergoes the hardships of actions he performs with no concern but to acquire the promised station therein and to master the result of the action — such as the efficacy of the will, for example — while he is in heedlessness of the aforementioned matter of the soul from the time he begins his work until he concludes it."

"Furthermore, among these people are those who see the soul as nothing but a material, natural thing like blood, or the vaporous spirit (الروح البخاري), or the original parts; and those who see the soul as a subtle body (جسم لطيف) resembling the elemental body and dwelling within it, being the carrier of life. How then is it permissible to say that everyone aims thereby at the matter of self-knowledge?"

However, you should remember what was previously mentioned: that the human being, in all these positions where he performs actions that divert the soul from preoccupation with external matters and diverse material enjoyments toward itself to obtain properties and effects that material causes and ordinary natural factors cannot reach, intends nothing but separation from external causes and independence with his self to obtain specific results that ordinary material factors have no way to.

The religious person who is ascetic in his religion sees that it is a human duty to choose for himself his true happiness, which is the good life of the Hereafter for those who believe in the Resurrection (المعاد), and the happy worldly life that gathers good for him and repels evil for those who deny it, such as the idolaters and the adherents of transmigration. Then he sees that indulgence in animalistic enjoyments does not secure his happiness nor lead him to his goal. Thus, he has no choice but to reject whims and cease the pursuit of everything his soul craves through its ordinary causes in general, and to be drawn toward a cause or causes above the ordinary material causes by drawing near to it and connecting with it. He sees that this proximity and connection are only attained by submission to it and surrender to its command, and that is a spiritual, psychological matter (أمر روحي نفساني) that is only preserved through bodily actions and abstentions. This is religious worship (العبادة الدينية), consisting of prayer, ritual, or what pertains to that.

Thus, religious actions, struggles (المجاهدات), and disciplines all return to a kind of preoccupation with the matter of the soul. The human being sees by primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — that he does not take anything nor leave anything except for the benefit of his self. It has already been stated that the human being is never devoid, even for a moment of his existence, of observing his self and the presence of his essence (حضور ذاته), and that he never errs in this consciousness at all. If he errs, he only errs in its interpretation according to theoretical opinion and intellectual research. Thus, it has become clear through this explanation that religions and doctrines, despite the diversity of their practices and paths, aim at nothing but preoccupation with the matter of the soul in general, whether their adherents know it or not.

Likewise, the practitioner of disciplines and struggles, even if he is not an adherent of a religion nor a believer in the reality of the soul, intends by the type of discipline he undergoes nothing but the acquisition of its promised result. The promised result is not linked to the actions and abstentions he performs by a natural link similar to the link existing between natural causes and their effects; rather, it is a volitional, non-material link related to the consciousness of the practitioner and his will, which are preserved by the type of action he performs, circulating between the soul of the practitioner and the promised result. Thus, the reality of the mentioned discipline is the strengthening of the soul and its perfection in its consciousness and will for the required result. If you wish, you could say: the effect of the discipline is that the soul obtains a state of knowledge (العلم) that the objective is within its power.
