When the discipline is sound and complete, it becomes such that if the soul wills the objective absolutely, or wills it under specific conditions — such as summoning the spirit for a pre-pubescent boy in a mirror — the objective is achieved.

To this category belongs the meaning of what is narrated: **أنه ذكر عند النبي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله : أن بعض أصحاب عيسى عليه‌السلام كان يمشي على الماء ـ فقال صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله : لو كان يقينه أشد من ذلك لمشى على الهواء** — "It was mentioned in the presence of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that some of the companions of Jesus (عليه السلام) used to walk on water. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Had his certainty (يقينه) been stronger than that, he would have walked on air.'"

The hadith — as you see — points out that the matter revolves around certainty (اليقين) in Allah, the Glorified, and the erasure of cosmic causes from independent influence. To whatever extent a person's reliance on the absolute divine power reaches, things will submit to him according to that measure; so understand that.

Among the most comprehensive sayings in this regard is the saying of Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): **ما ضعف بدن عما قويت عليه النية** — "A body does not weaken from what the intention (النية) is strong upon." And the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said in the mutawatir hadith: **إنما الأعمال بالنيات** — "Actions are only by intentions."

It has thus become clear that the religious effects of actions and worships, as well as the effects of disciplines and struggles, only establish their link between them and the human soul through its inner affairs. Therefore, engaging in any of them is an engagement with the matter of the soul.

Whoever imagines that the link of causality (السببية والمسببية) is only between the physical forms of these actions and the otherworldly goals, for example — such as ease, fragrance, and the Garden of Bliss — or between them and the strange worldly goals in which natural causes do not operate — such as influencing the perceptions of souls and types of their wills, movements without a mover, knowledge of inner thoughts and future events, and connection with spiritual beings and spirits, and the like — or imagines that the action entails the effect without a real link or by mere divine will without a specificator, has indeed deceived himself.

6 — Beware lest the matter become confused for you, leading you to conclude from the previous discussions that religion is gnosis (العرفان) and Sufism (التصوف) — meaning self-knowledge — as some materialist researchers have imagined, dividing the life path prevalent among people into two parts: materialism and gnosis, which is religion.

This is because what religion is based upon is that the human being has a true happiness (سعادة حقيقية) that he cannot attain except by submission to what is beyond nature and rejecting the limitation to material enjoyments. The previous discussions have produced the result that religions — whatever they may be, true or false — employ in the cultivation of people and leading them to the happiness they promise and invite them to, the reform and refinement of the soul in a manner suitable for the objective. Where is this from the claim that self-knowledge is religion?

Religion calls to the worship of the Deity, the Glorified, without a mediator or through intercessors and partners, because in it lies human happiness and the good life (الحياة الطيبة) beyond which there is no goal for man. Man does not and will not attain it except with a soul that is pure and purified from the stains of attachment to material things and unbridled animalistic enjoyments. Thus, the need arose to include within the parts of its call the reform and purification of the soul, so that the adherent raised in its lap may be prepared to be clothed in goodness and happiness, and not be like one who takes a thing with one hand and pushes it away with the other. Thus, religion is one thing, and self-knowledge is another thing beyond it, even if religion necessitates gnosis in a certain way.

By a similar explanation, it becomes clear that the paths of discipline and struggle followed for various purposes strange to habit are also other than self-knowledge, even if some are linked to others in a certain way.

Yes, we can conclude one thing: that self-knowledge (عرفان النفس), by whatever path its pursuit is assumed, is a matter taken from religion. Just as exhaustive and free research shows that religions, despite their diversity and dispersion, have branched out from a single, ancient religion to which the human primordial nature (الفطرة) invites, which is the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد).

For if we return to our simple primordial nature, disregarding the prejudices that have occurred to us through inheritance from our ancestors or contagion from our peers, we would not doubt that the world — despite its unity in its multiplicity and the connection of its parts in the very essence of their dispersion — ends at a single cause above all causes. This is the Truth (الحق) to whose side submission is obligatory, and according to whose management and cultivation the life path must be arranged. This is the religion based on monotheism.

Deep contemplation of all religions and sects shows that they contain this living spirit in some way, even idolatry and dualism. The difference only occurred in the application of the religious practice to this principle, and in being correct or erroneous regarding it. Some say, for example: He is closer to us than the jugular vein, and He is with us wherever we are, and we have no guardian or intercessor besides Him; thus, it is obligatory to worship Him alone without association. Others say: The terrestrial debasement of man and the vileness of his essence do not leave him a way to connect with that Presence — and where is the dust compared to the Lord of lords? Thus, it is obligatory that we draw near to some of His noble servants who are stripped of the cloak of matter and are pure and purified from the stains of nature; these are the spiritualities of the planets, or the lords of species (أرباب الأنواع), or those close to man, and **ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللهِ زُلْفى** — "We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah" (al-Zumar: 3).

Since they are absent from our senses and transcendent beyond our directions, it was necessary that we embody them with monuments and idols so that the matter of worshipful proximity may be completed. By this analogy, in other religions and nations, we find in their cores nothing but what is, in reality, a way of directing toward the monotheism of the Deity, exalted is His Name.

It is well known that the practices circulating among people, however they may branch out and however intensely they may differ, tend toward unity if we return to their previous eras, and end finally at the religion of the simple human primordial nature, which is monotheism. Thus, the religion of monotheism is the father of religions, and they are its children, whether righteous or corrupt.

Furthermore, the primordial religion only considers the matter of self-knowledge to reach through it the human happiness to which it invites, which is the knowledge of the Deity (معرفة الإله), which is the ultimate objective for it. In other words, religion only invites to self-knowledge as a path-oriented invitation (دعوة طريقية), not as an end-oriented one (غائية). For the religious taste does not accept preoccupation with a matter except in the way of servitude (العبودية). Indeed, the religion with Allah is Islam, and He does not approve of disbelief for His servants; how then would He approve of self-knowledge if it were independent in its desirability?

From here it appears that gnosis (العرفان) ends at the origin of the primordial religion, as it is not in itself an independent matter to which the human primordial nature invites, such that its branches and boughs would end at a single origin which is primordial gnosis.

It is possible to find support for this in another matter: that although humanity was driven by primordial nature toward society and civilization to make life happy, and transmission and research have established that men or social groups called to national ways or established social practices and implemented them among their nations — such as tribal practices, monarchical practices, democracy, and the like — it has not been established by transmission or research that anyone other than the people of religion has called to self-knowledge and the refinement of character throughout human history.

Yes, it is possible that some practitioners of these non-religious paths — such as the masters of sorcery and spirits and the like — only became aware of this type of self-knowledge through a path other than religion, but not from the direction of primordial nature, for primordial nature has no judgment in that as you have known. Rather, it was from the direction of observing some strange psychological effects by chance, so the soul yearns to attain a psychological station by which it possesses wondrous actions and rare influences in the cosmos that souls find strange. This yearning then drives it to search for it and journey toward it, and then journey after journey paves the way to the objective and eases the ruggedness of it.

7 — It is narrated from many of our righteous people among the people of religion that they attained, during their religious struggles, supernatural miracles (كرامات خارقة للعادة) and strange events that were specific to them among their peers — such as the representation of matters to their sight that were hidden from the sight of others, the observation of persons or events that the senses of people below them do not observe, the answering of supplication, the healing of the sick for whom there is no hope of successful treatment, and salvation from dangers and perils through non-ordinary ways. Similar things may happen to those other than the righteous if they possess a sincere intention and a detached soul (نفس منقطعة). These people see what they see while being heedless of its proximate cause; they only attribute that to Allah, the Glorified, without the mediation of a medium. Attributing matters to Him, the Exalted, although it is a truth that must be acknowledged, the denial of intermediate causes (الأسباب المتوسطة) is something for which there is no hope.

Sometimes a spiritualist summons the spirit of one of the people in a mirror or water and the like by influencing the soul of a boy — as is customary. He, like others, sees that the boy only perceives it with physical sight, and that between the sight of other observers and the summoned spirit there is a drawn veil which, if removed, would make them like the boy in attaining its observation.

Sometimes they find that the summoned spirits lie in their reports, which is strange because the world of spirits is a world of purity and clarity where there is no way for lying, fabrication, or falsehood.

Sometimes they summon the spirit of a living person and interrogate it regarding its secrets and inner thoughts, while the owner of the spirit is in a state of wakefulness, occupied with his daily tasks and needs, having no news that his spirit is summoned and interrogated, uttering speech that he would not approve of uttering.

Sometimes a person is put into a hypnotic sleep and then indoctrinated with an act until he graciously accepts it. When he is awakened and goes about his business, he performs the act he was indoctrinated with according to the condition intended for it, while being heedless of what they indoctrinated him with and of his gracious acceptance of it.

Some spiritualists, when they observed spiritual images resembling human images or images of some animals, thought that these images are in the world of matter and the vessel of changing nature — especially some of those who do not see existence for anything other than material matters. Some of these even tried to invent industrial tools to catch spirits, all based on a hypothesis they assumed regarding the soul: that it is a material principle or a property of a material principle that acts with consciousness and will, even though they have not solved the problem of life and consciousness to this day.

Similar to this hypothesis is the hypothesis of those who see that the spirit is a subtle body (جسم لطيف) resembling the elemental body in its forms and shapes, because they found that a person sees himself in a dream while in his form of wakefulness. Sometimes the images of their own selves are represented to the masters of struggles in front of them outside their bodies, perfectly resembling the bodily image. They thus judged that the spirit is a subtle body dwelling in the elemental body as long as the human being is alive, and when it departs the body, that is death.

They missed the fact that this is a perceptual image (صورة إدراكية) subsisting in the consciousness of the human being, similar to the image he perceives of his body, and similar to the images of all other external things separate from his body. Sometimes this separate image appears to some masters of struggle as more than one, or in a form other than the form of his own self. Sometimes he sees himself as the very self of another individual. If they do not judge these mentioned images to be the image of the spirit, then it is appropriate for them not to judge the single resembling image that appears to the masters of struggles to be the image of the spirit.

The reality of the matter is that these people attained something of the knowledges of the soul but missed the knowledge of its reality as it is; thus, they erred in interpreting what they attained and went astray in directing its matter. The truth to which proof and experience lead is that the reality of the soul — which is this rational consciousness (الشعور المتعقل) expressed by our saying "I" — is a matter distinct in its essence (جوهره) from these material matters as previously stated. Furthermore, the categories of its consciousness and types of its perceptions — whether sense, imagination, or intellection — from the aspect of being perceived objects, are established in its world and vessel, other than the natural properties occurring in the organs of sense and perception of the body, for those are material acts and passions devoid in themselves of life and consciousness. Thus, these observed matters specific to the righteous and the masters of struggles and disciplines are not outside the domain of their souls. The question is only how these pieces of information and knowledges were established in the soul and where their place is within it? And that the soul has a causal mark (سمة علية) for all events and matters linked to it in some way. All these strange matters that submit to the people of discipline and struggle only draw nourishment from their will and wish, and the will arises from consciousness. Thus, human consciousness has an entry into all events linked to it and matters touching it.

8 — It is appropriate to divide those engaged in self-knowledge in general into two groups: one group consists of those engaged in attaining some of the strange effects of the soul that are outside the normal range of material causes and effects, such as the masters of sorcery, talismans, and those engaged in the subjugation of the spiritualities of the planets and those entrusted with affairs, jinn, and human spirits, and the masters of supplications and incantations, and the like.

The second group consists of those engaged in self-knowledge by turning away from matters external to it and being drawn toward it to delve into it and observe its essence and its affairs, such as the Sufis (المتصوفة) in their various ranks and paths.

Sufism is not something that Muslims invented from themselves, as it is found among nations that preceded them in emergence, such as the Christians and others, even the idolaters among the Brahmins and Buddhists. Among them are those who follow the path to this day; rather, it is an inherited path they inherited from their ancestors.

However, this is not in the sense of ordinary taking and imitation, like people inheriting the colors of civilization from one another — a later nation from an earlier one — as several researchers in religions and doctrines have claimed. This is because you have known in the previous chapters that the primordial religion leads to asceticism, and asceticism guides to self-knowledge. Thus, the establishment of religion among a nation and its taking root in their hearts prepares and readies them for a path of self-knowledge to inevitably emerge among them. Some of those for whom the necessitating factors are complete will take it up. Thus, the remaining of religious life in a nation for a significant period inevitably creates among them this path, whether sound or corrupt, even if they are entirely cut off from other religious nations. That which is of this nature should not be counted among the inherited practices that one generation takes from another.

9 — Then we should divide the adherents of the second group of the two previous groups — who are the people of gnosis in reality — into two groups:

One group follows the path for its own sake, so they are granted something of its knowledges without the complete knowledge of it being finalized for them. This is because, since they do not intend other than the soul, they are in heedlessness of the matter of its Maker, who is Allah (عز اسمه), the True Cause who takes hold of the forelock of the soul in its existence and the effects of its existence. How can a person attain complete knowledge of a thing while being oblivious to the knowledge of the causes of its existence, especially the Cause who is the Cause of every cause? Is he not like one who claims knowledge of the bed while being ignorant of the carpenter, his adze, his saw, and his purpose in making it, among other causes of the bed's existence?

It is appropriate for this type of self-knowledge to be called divination (كهانة) due to what it entails of obtaining some of the sciences of the soul and its effects.

The other group intends the path of self-knowledge to be a means for them to the knowledge of the Lord, the Exalted. Their path is the one that religion approves of in general, which is that a person engages in the knowledge of his self insofar as it is a sign (آية) among the signs of his Lord, and the closest sign. Thus, the soul is a traversed path, and Allah, the Glorified, is the Goal toward which one journeys: **وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى** — "and that the terminus is toward your Lord" (al-Najm: 42).

These are various groups with dispersed doctrines among nations and sects. We do not have much expertise in the doctrines of non-Muslims among them and the paths they follow. As for the Muslims, their paths in this are many, perhaps reaching twenty-five chains (سلسلة) according to the origins, from each of which other particular chains branch out. They have traced them back — except for one — to Ali (عليه أفضل السلام). There are men among them who do not belong to any of these chains and are called the Uwaysis (أويسية) — in reference to Uways al-Qarani — and there are others among them who do not take a name nor manifest a slogan.

They have written books and treatises in which they have translated their chains and paths, the laws and etiquettes they have, and their men. They have recorded therein what is narrated of their unveilings (مكاشفاتهم) and expressed their proofs and objectives upon which they built them. Whoever wishes to stand upon them should refer to them. As for the research into the details of the paths and ways, and the correction of the sound and the critique of the corrupt, it has another place. A discussion that is not devoid of benefit in this regard has already preceded in the fifth volume of this book. This is the summary of what we intended to present of the research related to the meaning of self-knowledge.

Know that self-knowledge is a practical quest (بغية عملية) for which complete knowledge is not obtained except through the path of practical journeying (السلوك العملي) rather than the theoretical. As for the psychology (علم النفس) that the masters of theory among the ancients compiled, it does not avail anything in this regard. Likewise, the practical art of the soul that the moderns have recently compiled is only a branch of the art of ethics (فن الأخلاق) as the ancients compiled it. And Allah is the Guide.
